learning_objectives: Trace the history of learning theory from Thorndike through modern approaches
Define habituation and sensitization as basic forms of learning
Explain the adaptive significance of learning from an evolutionary perspective
readings: Domjan, The Principles of Learning and Behavior, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Learning is the process by which experience produces enduring changes in behavior or mental representations. This course examines the principles, mechanisms, and applications of learning and memory in depth.
key_concepts: Thorndike's law of effect: the origin of reinforcement theory
Habituation as the simplest form of learning: decreased response to repeated stimuli
Sensitization: increased response following an intense stimulus
Dual-process theory of habituation and sensitization
Evolutionary constraints on learning: biological preparedness
lecture_notes.demonstration: Habituation demonstration: repeated presentation of a startling sound, tracking galvanic skin response decline

review.value: Behaviors followed by satisfying consequences are strengthened; behaviors followed by annoying consequences are weakened
Organisms are evolutionarily predisposed to learn some associations more readily than others (e.g., taste-illness vs. taste-shock)

learning_objectives: Describe acquisition, extinction, spontaneous recovery, and stimulus generalization in classical conditioning
Explain the Rescorla-Wagner model of associative learning
Apply classical conditioning principles to real-world phenomena: phobias, drug tolerance, advertising

lecture_notes.introduction: Pavlov's discovery launched the scientific study of learning. Classical conditioning reveals how organisms learn predictive relationships between stimuli in their environment.
key_concepts: US, UR, CS, CR: the basic elements
Acquisition curve, extinction, spontaneous recovery
Stimulus generalization and discrimination
Rescorla-Wagner model: learning occurs when events are surprising (prediction error)
Blocking effect: prior conditioning prevents learning about a redundant cue
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conditioned emotional response demonstration using mild startle paradigm

review.value: Learning is driven by the discrepancy between what is expected and what occurs; no surprise = no learning (Rescorla-Wagner)
Kamin's blocking: a previously conditioned CS prevents conditioning of a new CS paired with the same US, because the US is already predicted

learning_objectives: Distinguish between positive/negative reinforcement and positive/negative punishment
Describe the four basic reinforcement schedules and their characteristic response patterns
Explain shaping, chaining, and applications of operant conditioning

lecture_notes.introduction: Operant conditioning examines how behavior is shaped by its consequences. Skinner's analysis of reinforcement remains foundational to behavioral psychology, education, and behavior modification.
key_concepts: Three-term contingency: antecedent, behavior, consequence
Fixed-ratio, variable-ratio, fixed-interval, variable-interval schedules
Differential reinforcement and shaping (successive approximation)
Premack principle: high-probability behaviors reinforce low-probability behaviors
Behavioral economics: matching law and delay discounting
lecture_notes.demonstration: Shaping exercise: use operant principles to train a classmate to perform a novel behavior using only verbal reinforcement

review.value: Reinforcement after an unpredictable number of responses; produces high, steady response rates and maximum resistance to extinction (e.g., gambling)
Organisms allocate responses in proportion to the relative rate of reinforcement available on each alternative

class_schedule.topic: Memory Systems: Encoding, Storage, and Retrieval
learning_objectives: Compare declarative and non-declarative memory systems with supporting evidence
Explain the testing effect and desirable difficulties in memory
Describe forgetting: decay, interference, and retrieval failure theories

lecture_notes.introduction: Memory research has revealed that memory is not a single entity but a collection of systems with different neural substrates, operating principles, and time courses.
key_concepts: Declarative (explicit): episodic and semantic memory
Non-declarative (implicit): procedural, priming, conditioning
Testing effect (retrieval practice): testing strengthens memory more than restudying
Desirable difficulties: spacing, interleaving, generation enhance long-term retention
Interference theory: proactive and retroactive interference
lecture_notes.demonstration: Testing effect experiment: students study material using restudying vs. retrieval practice, then compare retention at end of session
setup_requirements: Study materials for testing effect demonstration

review.value: Practicing retrieval (self-testing) produces stronger long-term memory than equal time spent restudying; one of the most robust findings in memory research
Distributing study over time produces better retention than massing study into a single session, even with equal total study time

learning_objectives: Explain the constructive nature of memory and schema-based distortions
Describe the misinformation effect and its implications for eyewitness testimony
Evaluate the recovered memory debate and DRM paradigm findings
readings: Baddeley et al., Ch. 11-12; Loftus, 'Make-Believe Memories' (Scientific American)

lecture_notes.introduction: Memory is not a video recording. It is a reconstructive process that blends actual experience with post-event information, schemas, and imagination.
key_concepts: Bartlett's schema theory: remembering as reconstruction
Misinformation effect (Loftus): post-event information alters memory reports
DRM paradigm: false recognition of semantically related lure words
Source monitoring errors: confusing imagined events with real ones
Implications for legal system: eyewitness confidence is weakly related to accuracy
lecture_notes.demonstration: DRM false memory demonstration: present word lists and test for recall of critical lures

review.value: Loftus showed that misleading post-event questions alter witnesses' memory reports: 'How fast were the cars going when they smashed?' vs. 'contacted'
Errors occur when people remember the content of an experience but misattribute its source (e.g., believing they saw something they only imagined)

learning_objectives: Apply evidence-based study strategies derived from learning and memory research
Discuss the application of conditioning principles in clinical settings (exposure therapy, token economies)
Evaluate educational practices in light of cognitive science findings
readings: Brown, Roediger, & McDaniel, Make It Stick, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: The science of learning and memory has direct practical applications in education, clinical psychology, and everyday life. This session bridges laboratory findings to real-world practice.
key_concepts: Retrieval practice, spaced repetition, and interleaving as optimal study strategies
Exposure therapy as applied extinction: inhibitory learning model
Token economies and applied behavior analysis (ABA)
Desirable difficulties framework for instruction design
Metacognitive illusions: fluency vs. actual learning
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students design an evidence-based study plan for a hypothetical exam using retrieval practice, spacing, and interleaving principles

review.value: Extinction does not erase the original CS-US association but creates a new inhibitory association; explains renewal, reinstatement, and spontaneous recovery
Students often confuse processing fluency (ease of reading) with learning; rereading feels effective but produces weak retention compared to testing

questions.question: In the Rescorla-Wagner model, explain why a blocking effect occurs. Use the concept of prediction error in your answer and provide a concrete example.
questions.answer: The Rescorla-Wagner model states that learning depends on the discrepancy between what is expected and what occurs (prediction error). In blocking, Phase 1 trains CS_A to fully predict the US, so its associative strength reaches asymptote. In Phase 2, CS_A and CS_B are presented together with the same US. Because CS_A already fully predicts the US, the prediction error is zero (expected = actual), so CS_B gains no associative strength. Example: if a rat learns that a tone (A) predicts food, then adding a light (B) alongside the tone still predicts the same food. The food is not surprising, so the rat learns nothing about the light.

rubric.key: Correctly explains prediction error as the learning mechanism
Explains why CS_B gains no strength when US is already predicted

questions.question: A parent wants to reduce a child's tantrum behavior. Explain why negative reinforcement and positive punishment are often confused, define each precisely, and recommend which operant strategy would be most effective for tantrum reduction.
questions.answer: Negative reinforcement INCREASES behavior by removing an aversive stimulus (e.g., giving in to tantrums reinforces them because the aversive tantrum stops when the parent complies). Positive punishment DECREASES behavior by adding an aversive consequence. They are confused because both involve aversive stimuli, but they have opposite effects on behavior. For tantrum reduction, the most effective evidence-based strategy is extinction (planned ignoring): withholding the reinforcement that maintains the behavior. Combined with differential reinforcement of alternative behavior (DRA), the parent reinforces appropriate communication while withholding reinforcement for tantrums.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of negative reinforcement and positive punishment
Explains why they are commonly confused

questions.question: Describe the evidence from amnesic patients that supports the distinction between declarative and non-declarative memory systems.
questions.answer: Patient H.M. (and others with bilateral medial temporal lobe damage) could not form new declarative (explicit) memories -- episodic events or semantic facts. However, they could learn new motor skills (mirror tracing), show priming effects, and acquire classically conditioned responses. This demonstrates that non-declarative (implicit) memory systems operate independently of the hippocampal system required for declarative memory. The double dissociation is further supported by patients with basal ganglia damage (Huntington's disease) who have impaired procedural learning but intact declarative memory.

rubric.key: Notes double dissociation with procedural memory patients

questions.question: Explain the DRM false memory paradigm and discuss what it reveals about the constructive nature of memory.
questions.answer: In the DRM (Deese-Roediger-McDermott) paradigm, participants study lists of words semantically associated with a non-presented critical lure (e.g., 'bed, rest, awake, tired, dream' associated with 'sleep'). On a subsequent recognition test, participants frequently and confidently 'remember' the critical lure, even though it was never presented. This reveals that memory is constructive, not reproductive: the brain extracts gist and meaning rather than recording exact details. Activation spreading through semantic networks during encoding creates a strong memory trace for the associated concept. Participants cannot distinguish this internally generated activation from externally presented words.

rubric.key: Accurately describes the DRM procedure and results
Explains the constructive nature of memory it demonstrates
Discusses spreading activation or gist-based processing

major_assignments.title: Evidence-Based Learning Intervention Design
major_assignments.description: Design an evidence-based learning intervention for a specific real-world context (e.g., classroom instruction, clinical behavior change, workplace training). Ground the intervention in learning and memory research, specify procedures in detail, and propose an evaluation plan.
objectives: Apply learning and memory principles to a practical problem
Design a detailed intervention protocol grounded in research evidence
Communicate the intervention design clearly and persuasively
deliverables: 3,000-word intervention design paper in APA format
Detailed protocol with materials and procedures
Evaluation plan with proposed measures and analyses

criteria: Appropriate application of principles to the chosen context
Strong theoretical grounding in learning and memory research
Rigorous evaluation plan with appropriate methodology

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of learning and memory concepts

midterm_examination.description: Exam covering conditioning and basic memory processes

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all learning and memory topics

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance is expected. Lab sessions requiring data collection are mandatory.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Lab reports must be submitted by the following class session.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed collaboratively. All written assignments must be independently authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. Fabrication of experimental data is a serious violation.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to learning and memory experiment software, SPSS or JASP, and university library databases.
